coredata.dc:title: Carbon-based 0D/1D/2D assembly with desired structures and defect states as non-metal bifunctional electrocatalyst for zinc-air battery

coredata.dc:description: For the design of electrocatalysts, the combination between components and the regulation of material structures tend to be neglected, giving rise to the constraint of catalytic performance and durability. Herein, we developed a graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) with enhanced oxygen content by a one-step cutting method. Then, one-dimensional (1D) carbon nanotubes and two-dimensional (2D) reduced graphene oxide are crosslinked and self-assembled, thus attracting unsaturated-bond-riches GOODs (0D) to uniformly attach to the skeleton, simultaneously achieving nitrogen and sulfur co-doping. To the best of our knowledge, there is no report to prepare bifunctional electrocatalyst with GOQDs. Electrochemical tests show that even without metal-doping, the novel non-metal bifunctional electrocatalyst (N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT) exhibits a higher half-wave potential (0.84 V) and enhanced limiting current density (5.88 mA cm−2) than commercial Pt/C catalyst. The density functional theory is implemented to reveal the coordination of nitrogen and sulfur co-doping on GOQDs, which results in the improvement of overall catalytic active sites. Furthermore, the rechargeable zinc-air battery based on N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT exhibits a maximum power density of 134.3 mW cm−2, open circuit potential of 1.414 V, which is better than Pt/C+Ru/C mixed material. The obtained N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT will provide a perspective application in fuel cells.

(d) AFM image of GOQDs (inset: a photo of GOQDs solution) and (e) the corresponding histogram of height distribution. (f) the height distribution along the line AB in (d). (g) FT-IR spectra of GO and GOQDs, and h) high-resolution XPS C1s spectrum of GOQDs.

(a) XRD patterns and (b) Raman spectra of RGO, N,S-RGO, N,S-RGO/CNT, and N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT. (c) N2 adsorption and desorption isotherms of N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT (Inset: calculated pore-size distribution). (d) Survey XPS spectrum of N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT and corresponding high-resolution (e) C 1s, (f) N 1s, and (g) S 2p spectra. Electrostatic potential plots of N,S co-doping graphene structure with different ratio of pyridine N/graphite N (h) 1:1, (i) 2:1 and (j) 1:2.

(a) LSV curves of N,S-RGO, N,S-RGO/CNT, N-GOQD-RGO/CNT, S-GOQD-RGO/CNT, N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT and Pt/C samples tested at 1600 rpm with a scan rate of 10 mV s−1 in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH. (b) ORR Tafel plots for N,S-RGO, N,S-RGO/CNT, N-GOQD-RGO/CNT, S-GOQD-RGO/CNT, N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT and Pt/C. (c) Half-wave potential histogram of all 8 materials. (d) LSV curve of N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT obtained at different rotation speeds (rpm) with a scan rate of 10 mV s−1. (e) K-L association calculated in the range of 0.3–0.6 V at different rotational speeds. (f) Electrochemical impedance spectra of Pt/C and N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT in 0.1 M KOH solution over the frequency range from 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz.

(b)LSV curves of N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT after 0, 2000, 4000 and 5000 cycles. (c) chronoamperometric stability plots of N,S‐GOQD-RGO/CNT and 20 wt% Pt/C in 0.1 MKOH. head.ce:title: Carbon-based 0D/1D/2D assembly with desired structures and defect states as non-metal bifunctional electrocatalyst for zinc-air battery

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: For the design of electrocatalysts, the combination between components and the regulation of material structures tend to be neglected, giving rise to the constraint of catalytic performance and durability. Herein, we developed a graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) with enhanced oxygen content by a one-step cutting method. Then, one-dimensional (1D) carbon nanotubes and two-dimensional (2D) reduced graphene oxide are crosslinked and self-assembled, thus attracting unsaturated-bond-riches GOODs (0D) to uniformly attach to the skeleton, simultaneously achieving nitrogen and sulfur co-doping. To the best of our knowledge, there is no report to prepare bifunctional electrocatalyst with GOQDs. Electrochemical tests show that even without metal-doping, the novel non-metal bifunctional electrocatalyst (N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT) exhibits a higher half-wave potential (0.84 V) and enhanced limiting current density (5.88 mA cm−2) than commercial Pt/C catalyst. The density functional theory is implemented to reveal the coordination of nitrogen and sulfur co-doping on GOQDs, which results in the improvement of overall catalytic active sites. Furthermore, the rechargeable zinc-air battery based on N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT exhibits a maximum power density of 134.3 mW cm−2, open circuit potential of 1.414 V, which is better than Pt/C+Ru/C mixed material. The obtained N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT will provide a perspective application in fuel cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Platinum carbon (Pt/C) composites are widely used in conventional fuel batteries and rechargeable zinc-air batteries due to the good oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) catalytic performance. Although platinum has good ORR catalytic performance, it requires a large amount of load to ensure efficiency. More critically, platinum is expensive and is accompanied by the degradation of catalytic stability [1]. Thus, the large-scale applications of fuel cells are greatly hindered by the high costs and low durability [2]. Carbon-based nanomaterials have considerable potential in the area of electrocatalytic owing to their rich nanostructures which could be further processed in various approaches, such as element doping, interaction with specific species. Meanwhile, carbon-based materials are cheap, which could reduce the costs compared to precious metal catalysts [3]. In particular, benefiting from the combinations and mutual coupling between different heteroatoms, the heteroatom-doped graphene exhibits improved electrocatalytic activity and stability [4]. Nitrogen-doped graphene has been considered as a promising catalyst. Nitrogen atoms with five valence electrons tend to form strong valence bonds with carbon atoms. The unpaired electrons in nitrogen atoms can provide electrocatalytically active sites after hybridization with the sp2 hybrid orbit in carbon atom on graphene skeleton, which significantly improves the reactivity and electrocatalytic performance of graphene [5]. Furthermore, nitrogen/sulfur dual-doped graphene could synergistically improve the electrocatalytic properties because the electronegativity difference between nitrogen and sulfur produced more catalytically active sites [6–10]. In addition to heteroatom doping, size effects and structure influences of the composites are critical to the improvement of catalytic activity [11].

Some researches have proposed an improved catalyst of carbon-based materials by combining 0D quantum dots such as carbon quantum dots and graphene quantum dots to matrix composites. However, the electrocatalytic performances were much lower than that of precious metal catalysts [12–15]. The lower electrocatalytic performances may be resulting from the low preparation efficiency and unstable quality of quantum dots during the “bottom to up” method or the electrochemical synthesis process. Moreover, quantum dots prepared by “up to bottom” are faced with long post-treatment processes, such as more than a week of dialysis cleaning time, or a complicated purification process. Additionally, the size distribution and functional groups of quantum dots are strongly sensitive to the synthesis process, which will affect the bonding stability between atoms during the catalyst synthesis process. The stacking of substrate and aggregation of active sites during synthesis processes can lead to a decrease of catalytic performance [16–19]. Therefore, it is still a challenge to explore a facile method of preparing GOQDs with rich oxygen-containing functional groups.

Herein, we proposed an economically and stable non-metal catalyst with GOQDs. We developed a one-step hydrothermal approach to cut GO into GOQDs in acidic condition, where the optimum ratio of raw materials and the hydrothermal temperature was determined, and dramatically reduces the post-treatment processes. The uniformly dispersed GOQDs with abundant oxygen-containing functional groups have an extremely tiny diameter, which enables it to effectively bind to graphene/CNT skeleton through a large number of unsaturated bonds to promote electrocatalytic performance. During the synthesis process, GOQDs and GO belong to the original material, which provides high-quality nitrogen and sulfur doping sites while stably recombining. In particular, CNT effectively supports and hinders the stacking tendency towards graphene sheets, and crosslinks into a stable three-dimensional structure in the subsequent self-assembly process to help more active sites be exposed. By exploring and adjusting the structural dimensions and active sites of non-metallic catalysts, N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT bifunctional electrocatalyst is obtained, which shows higher catalytic stability and comparable electrocatalytic with commercial Pt/C+RuO2 in alkaline electrolytes. This work will provide the experimental and theoretical basis for the development of fuel cells and metal-air batteries.

Graphene oxide (GO) was prepared from flake graphite using Hummers method [8]. GOQDs were prepared by a one-pot hydrothermal process in a dark environment. 300 mg GO powder was added to H2SO4/HNO3 (volume ratio of 1:3) solution. The mixture was ultrasonicated for several hours to obtain the secondary oxidation GO. After centrifugation, the slurry was re-dispersed in HNO3 solution and then transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave to keep at 120 °C for 14 h. The GOQDs slurry was obtained after centrifugation at 12,000 rpm for 10 min and was re-dispersed in deionized water to form a stable solution. Then, residual HNO3 was neutralized with 3 M NaOH solution, the pH value of the solution was real-time monitored with a pH test paper until the pH value reached about 6.5. The solution was filtered using a 0.22 μm polytetrafluoroethylene microfiltration membranes to remove unreacted GO. Finally, light brown GOQDs dispersion solution was collected after dialysis for 20 h with regenerated cellulose membrane bag (MD44-1000DA). The concentration of the final solution was ~1.30 mg/ml.

50 mg GO and 10 mg O-CNTs powder was added into 40 mL GOQDs solution with ultrasonication for 0.5 h in dark environment. 70 mg melamine and 50 mg dibenzyl disulfide were added into the solution to obtain the complete dispersion. The mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and keep at 180 °C for 12 h to obtain the N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT hydrogel, which was freeze-dried to obtain the powder product. Annealing processes were carried out at 900 °C for X hours (X = 1, 2, 3) under Ar flow with 5 °C min−1 ramping rate. For comparison, we also prepared reduced graphene (RGO), nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene (N,S-RGO), nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene/CNT (N,S-RGO/CNT), nitrogen-doped graphene/CNT with GOQDs (N-GOQD-RGO/CNT) and sulfur-doped graphene/CNT with GOQDs (S-GOQD-RGO/CNT). The processes were similar to the synthesis of N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT, except that RGO was obtained directly after heat treatment at 900 °C for 1 h. The material after heat treatment was named (N,S)-(GOQD)-RGO/(CNT)@900-X (GOQDs and CNT were optional configurations), if not specified, “900-1” was abbreviated as “900”.

Electrochemical tests were performed at room temperature using CHI760D electrochemical workstation (Chenhua Instruments, Shanghai, China) assembled with arotating ring-disk electrode (RRDE-3A), which had a three-electrodes system, carbon rod as a counter electrode, Ag/AgCl as a reference electrode, and glassy carbon (GC) working electrode obtained by casting catalyst ink. After flowing O2 or N2 into the solution for at least 0.5 h, electrochemical tests were conducted in 0.1 M KOH. For the preparation of the active catalyst, 3 mg of sample material was added to 200 μL 25 vol% isopropanol solution, following sonication for 30 min, and ultrasonication for 30 min after the addition of 25 μL of Nafion solution (5.0 wt%). 2 μL blended ink mixture was pipetted onto the GC electrode (3 mm diameter) until it dried, which was the same as the preparation of 20% Pt/C catalyst (Sigma-Aldrich). The catalyst loading was about 0.37 mg cm−2. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was performed within a potential range of −0.8–0.2 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. Then, linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) tests were performed to investigate ORR performance. LSV curves at the potential range of −0.8–0.2 V with a scan rate of 10 mV s−1 and a rotation speed of 400, 625, 900, 1225, or 1600 rpm were measured in O2 saturated electrolyte. Keeping the same testing parameters, LSV curves for different speeds were measured under N2 saturated electrolyte (subtract background current to eliminate capacitive contributions). The stability test was thoroughly evaluated by four methods, where the CV cycle used a scan rate of 10 mV/s to get the closest actual data. OER measurements were carried out at 1600 rpm with a potential range of 0.0 to1.2 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) with a scan rate of 10 mV s−1. All samples were tested at least three times to achieve approximately performance. Converting the obtained Ag/AgCl potential to RHE potential according to the following relationship:

where J is the measured current density, which was calculated by geometric area of the electrode, J L and J K are diffusions and kinetic limiting current densities, respectively, ω is the angular rotation velocity of the disk, C 0 is the O2 concentration dissolved in KOH solution (1.2 × 10−6 mol cm−3), D 0 is the O2 diffusion coefficient in electrolyte (1.9 × 10−5 cm2 s−1), F is the Faraday’s constant (96,485 C mol−1), and V is kinetic viscosity of the KOH electrolyte solution (0.01 cm2 s−1). The value of B is reciprocal of the slope of fitted curve obtained by the K-L equation. The electron transfer number at different potentials was obtained by the relationship above, and finally take the average value.

A schematic diagram of the fabrication of GOQDs is given in Fig. 1 a. GO sheets are cut and oxidized by HNO3/H2SO4 solution, and GOQDs are obtained via centrifugation. A large number of GOQDs are evenly distributed on the TEM sample grid (Fig. 1b). By counting 200 particles, the average particle size is ~2.2 nm, and the proportion of GOQDs with the size between 1.2 and 2.5 nm accounts for ~85% (Fig. 1c). It is proved that ultrasonic vibration cooperating with cutting by HNO3 is an effective method to prepare GOQDs. In addition, the lattice spacing of GOQDs is circa 0.24 nm from the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image (the inset of Fig. 1b), which is consistent with other reports [20,21]. The raw solution (original concentration) was characterized by atomic force microscopy (AFM) to measure the height of GOODs. It can be seen from Fig. 1d that the GOQDs deposited on the mica plate are densely dispersed in the field of view. Together with the height distribution histogram in Fig. 1e and the local height line pattern along the line AB in Fig. 1f, it can be seen that the thickness of quantum dots is mainly distributed between 0.5 and 1.5 nm, which takes up ~84%. Thus, we conclude that the as-prepared GOQDs mainly correspond to 1–4 layers of graphene sheets according to the theoretical thickness of single-layer graphene of 0.34 nm [22,23]. The untrimmed TEM and AFM (Fig. S1 and Fig. S2) full distribution maps together prove the high-quality of GOQDs. In particular, each GOQDs is kept in a clean state, and there are no shadow impurities around it, which means that only one day of dialysis and purification can get pure GOQDs solution. Fig. S3 shows the PL spectra of GOQDs excited at different wavelengths. The excitation-wavelength-dependent emissions (500–565 nm) are observed, which stem from the radiative recombination of electron–hole pairs in the sp2 carbon clusters due to quantum confinement effects. These typical characteristics match well with the PL spectra from GQDs [58–60]. In order to further explore the surface state of GOQDs, the chemical bonding and functional group information in GO and GOQDs are characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). As shown in Fig. 1g, both samples exhibit strong and broad absorption peaks at 3430 cm−1, which is related to stretching vibration peak of -OH. The peaks at 2920 cm−1 and 2850 cm−1 are related to CH functional groups [24]. The as-prepared GOQDs manifest a series of characteristic peaks at 1726 cm−1, 1624 cm−1, 1380 cm−1, 1245 cm−1, and 1110 cm−1, which are related to the stretching vibration mode of CO and CC, bending vibration mode of COH, and stretching vibration mode of CO and COC, respectively [25,26]. Compared with GO, the peaks of characteristic oxygen-containing functional groups are significantly enhanced, indicating a great increase of oxygen-containing functional groups in GOQDs. The high-resolution X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of C1s are used to analyze oxygen content changes in GOQDs and GO quantitatively. As shown in Fig. 1h and Fig. S4, the peaks at 284.8, 286.8, 287.8 and 289 eV correspond to CC, CO, CO and OCO, respectively [27]. Considering the data of Table S1 and S2, a large amount of oxygen-containing functional groups are verified to form on the surface of GOQDs, where oxygen content increases from 35.8% to 45.26%. Thus, the GOQDs are easily adhered on GO sheets and conduce to dope with nitrogen and sulfur elements.

The preparation process of N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT composite material is illustrated in Fig. 2 a. Through self-assembly, the GOQDs (0D) are formed on the skeleton (3D) assembled by carbon nanotubes (1D) and graphene sheets (2D). CNTs effectively impede the stacking trend of graphene sheets. At the same time, GOQDs can modify the RGO/CNT skeleton, introduce many defect sites, and transform them to catalytic active sites in the subsequent process. Fig. S8 shows an SEM image of pure GO, which exhibits a typical wrinkled sheet structure. Fig. 2b and its inset are SEM images of N,S-RGO with different magnifications, which reveal a large number of fragmented graphene sheets with a wrinkled appearance. The fragmentation of graphene sheets is mainly due to the high-temperature heat treatment, while the wrinkle morphology is related to high quality sulfur doping [28]. The incorporation of sulfur increases the curl of graphene sheets and changes local charge distribution. SEM images of N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT material is shown in Fig. 2c and Fig. S9 (c, d). It can be observed that the CNTs have a certain degree of truncation and curl after acidification. Compared with Fig. S9 (a, b), the graphene sheets are transformed into a fluffy structure. CNT self-assembles with graphene sheets during hydrogel formation and the interlayer stacking is effectively suppressed. In addition to the spontaneously formed crosslinked structure, CNTs support graphene sheets and further form a stable base skeleton as an efficient charge transport channels of the catalyst. Based on a stable internal channel, the surface state is mainly completed by graphene and GOQDs doped with heteroatoms. In Fig. 2d, GOQDs are uniformly anchored at the edges and wrinkles of graphene layer, and the size of reduced quantum dots slightly decreases to ~1 nm mainly owing to the removal of oxygen-containing groups during hydrothermal and heat treatments [29]. The inset image stands for the enlarged morphology of quantum dots assembled on graphene sheets. A large number of defects and functional group interactions in GO and GOQDs determine the adhering process of GOQDs on GO sheets, which provides additional active sites, offers a smooth electron transfer path and enhances electrocatalytic performance. A wide-range TEM image of N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT is shown in Fig. 2e and the full range image without cropping is shown in Fig. S10. The element mapping shows uniform distribution of C, N, S, O elements in the composite material [30].

Fig. 3 a shows typical X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT and the other as-prepared materials. Characteristic diffraction peaks appear at 26° and 42°, which are related to the (002) and (100) planes of graphene, respectively. The dominant peak of N,S-RGO shifts to left because of the increased number of defects, which was introduced by nitrogen doping and successful incorporation of large radius sulfur. The dominant peak position of N,S-RGO/CNT is further shifted to the left. By the formula 2dsinθ = nλ, the interlayer spacing of N,S-RGO/CNT is ~ 0.34 nm, which is close to the layer spacing of the theoretical graphene of 0.34 nm [31]. It demonstrates that CNTs have a positive effect on inhibiting the stacking of graphene layers. Similarly, the dominant peak of N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT also shifts to the left due to the changes in the size and number of defects in graphene structure after adding GOQDs. The change in the full width at half maxima (FWHM) of the dominant XRD peak can explain the interaction between different effects of CNTs and quantum dots. The FWHM of N,S-RGO/CNT (0.676°) is significantly narrower than that N,S-RGO (2.407°), suggesting the higher crystallinity after CNT addition. The material added with quantum dots, N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT, has an FWHM of 1.518°, which is between the other two samples, indicating that the composite modified with graphene oxide quantum dots has amorphous nature and more surface defects. The structure and defect of all materials are analyzed by Raman spectroscopy in Fig. 3b. The D band at 1346 cm−1 is the disordered vibration peak of graphene, which is used to characterize structural defects and edge defects in graphene [32]. G band at 1589 cm−1 site is related to the plane vibration of SP2 carbon atoms [33]. The degree of the composite disorder can be characterized by ID/IG ratio, which increases from 0.85 for RGO to 1.05 for N,S-RGO, indicating that N,S-doping leads to a change in the graphene lattice and produces more defect structures [34]. The value of ID/IG for N,S-RGO/CNT decrease to 0.93 because more ordered SP2 C structures were introduced by combination with CNTs. However, ID/IG further increases to 1.17 after adding GOQDs to the composite. The reason may be that GOQDs increase surface defects by embedding in graphene sheet. It can be seen from Fig. 3b that the materials with CNTs have unique 2D peaks at 2697 cm−1 and 2954 cm−1, reflecting the few-layer graphene state of the composite [35,36], but the 2D peak appears in RGO due to a loose structure with thermal expansion. The widened 2D peak of N,S-RGO material means the aggregation of multi-layer graphene sheets. Nitrogen adsorption–desorption measurements (Fig. 3c) were performed to further explore the structure of the materials. N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT has a large specific Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) surface area of 409.84 m2/g. The steep N2 uptakes atP/P0 < 0.005 indicates the existence of rich micropores. WhenP/P0 = 0.45–0.95, pronounced hysteresis loops of Type Ⅳ suggest the presence of abundant mesopores. And the rapid uptakes of N2 atP/P0 > 0.95 prove the existence of macropores. Furthermore, the specific surface areas of N,S-RGO and N,S-RGO/CNT were determined to be 282.19 and 355.09 m2/g, respectively. The average pore diameter was calculated using the Barret–Joyner–Halenda (BJH) model, and the values of N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT, N,S-RGO/CNT, and N,S-RGO are 7.77, 9.35, and 9.75 nm (Fig. S11 and Table S3). It can be seen that N,S-RGO/CNT has a larger specific surface area than that of N,S-RGO, indicating CNTs can inhibit the stacking of graphene sheets and be beneficial for the formation of interconnected 3D structure. Additionally, compared to N,S-RGO, the material with GOQDs (N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT) has the largest specific surface area and the lowest average pore size, which is related to the simultaneous synthesis of GOQDs. The results above indicate that the N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT structure consists of a large number of micropores, abundant mesopores and exposed active sites. The synergistic effects of structure optimization and ultrafine GOQDs can be beneficial to enhance electrochemical performances.

XPS tests are used to study the properties of doping elements in the composite [37–41]. Nitrogen and sulfur co-doping contributes to the improvement of ORR performance. N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT still retains 4.334% N and 1.349% S after high-temperature heat treatment at 900 °C (Fig. 3d), indicating that an appropriate doping method and a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups of GOQDs increase doping level. Fig. 3e shows C 1 s spectrum of N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT, the binding energies of 284.74, 285.20, 286.34, and 291.00 eV are related to CC, CO, CO, and O-CO bonds, respectively. Among them, the fitting peak intensity increases because of overlap of the doping heteroatoms with the CO and CO bonds [42]. There are three peaks in N 1s spectrum (Fig. 3f): pyridinic N (398.4 eV), pyrrolic N (399.9 eV), and graphitic N (401.9 eV). S 2p spectrum in Fig. 3g shows four fitting peaks, where 163.8 and 165.15 eV correspond to the 2p1/2 and 2p3/2 spin-orbit bimodal of CSC bond. The peaks at 168.4 and 169.6 eV correspond to the 2p1/2 and 2p3/2 splitting of C-SO2-C, respectively [43,44]. The introduced C-SO2-C bond can effectively improve the ORR performance and promote the four-electron transfer process of ORR [45–48]. Fig. S12 shows the XPS spectra and Table S4 displays the element content of N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT, N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT-2 and N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT-3. Compared with N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT, nitrogen and sulfur contents decrease with the increase of heat treatment time, With the increase of heat treatment time, the proportion of graphitized N rises to 60.51%. Cooperating with the ORR catalytic performance test, we found a proper increase in the ratio of graphitic N can improve the ORR performance. However, excessive heat treatment reduces the amount of heteroatom and weakens synergistic effect of nitrogen and sulfur. This regular change also contributes to the overall doping regulation of materials. Fig. S13 shows high-resolution XPS spectra of N,S-RGO and N,S-RGO/CNT. Compared with N,S-RGO, graphene layer largely exposes after adding acidified CNTs, resulting in more heteroatom doping, which is quantified in Table S5. The nitrogen content increases from 1.705% to 2.139%, and the sulfur content increases from 0.658% to 1.697%. Besides, CNTs with a highly connected structure can effectively inhibit the interlayer stacking tendency caused by strong van der Waals force among graphene sheets. Consequently, by optimizing the structure and surfacestate of the catalyst and matching with heat treatment process, more catalytic active sites appear to improve catalytic performance.

Density functional theory calculations are used to evaluate the effect of N,S co-doping graphene structure with different pyridine/graphite N ratio on the ORR performance, and the optimal ratio between them can maximize the improvement of electrocatalytic performance. The detail of the model can be seen in Fig. S14 in supporting information. The calculations are performed with the Gaussian09 programs and the B3LYP functional of 6-31G (d, p) for all the atoms. [54] The contribution of bulk solvent effects using the polarizable continuum model. Based on XPS, three N,S co-doped graphene structures with the different pyridine/graphite N ratio are constructed to evaluate the catalytic performance corresponding to the experiment result. As shown in Fig. 3(h, i, j), the electrostatic potential energy diagrams of three structures with different pyridine/graphite N ratio are represented. The blue and red areas in the Electrostatic potential plots represent the positive and negative areas, respectively. The positive electrostatic potential corresponds to low electron density and shielding of the nuclear charge, repulsing a proton. From Fig. 3h, it can be seen that the scope of positive potential energy region is larger than that of negative areas when the ratio of pyridine nitrogen to graphite nitrogen is 1:1. It means that positive potential energy region might be the active sites to facilitate the adsorption of multielectron oxygen molecules in ORR performance. Compared with the Fig. 3h, when the ratio of pyridine nitrogen/graphite N is 2:1 or 1:2 in the graphene structure, the heteroatoms might break the charge balance of the covalent graphene electron system and disrupt the charge redistribution presenting a poor catalytic activity as shown in Fig. 3i and 3j. Furthermore, previous studies have shown that the step of OOH adsorption is most likely the rate-determining step [63,64]. To examine the structural change during the ORR process, the catalyst structures are optimized and the adsorption energy are quantitatively estimated after the adsorption of OOH intermediates onto different types of nitrogen doping configurations. As shown in Fig. S15, pyridine N/graphite N (1:1) shows an adsorption energy of −2.51 eV, which is much lower than other ratios of pyridine N/graphite N (1:2, −2.46 eV; 2:1, −1.73 eV). These results further confirm that N atom can induce more catalytic active sites when the ratio of pyridine nitrogen/graphite N is 1:1 (Heat treatment is 900 ℃ for 1 h) in the co-doped N/S graphene structure.

Through the above discussion, we further test to verify the specific improvement of the catalyst structure and surface state on the ORR catalytic performance, and the impact of these adjustments on heteroatom doping. We measured the electrocatalytic performances of all comparative samples and 20 wt% Pt/C (not IR-corrected). The linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves of the samples at 1600 rpm in O2-saturated conditions are shown in Fig. 4 a. Compared with N,S-RGO, the onset potential (Eonset) of N,S-RGO/CNT raises from 0.81 V to 0.84 V, and the half-wave potential (E1/2) raises from 0.62 V to 0.68 V (vs. RHE). It is evidenced that CNTs can optimize the structure and improve the ORR performance, but show little increase for the limiting current density. Surprisingly, the materials containing GOQDs exhibit a significant increase of limiting current density and optimized ORR performance after thermal treatment. In particular, the limiting current density of materials inlaid with GOQDs increases significantly, the maximum value is 5.88 mA cm−2, exceeding Pt/C material (4.74 mA cm−2), indicating that the addition of quantum dots and structural optimization significantly enhance electrical responses of the materials. GOQDs can improve mass transfer capability on the composite surface, leading to a stronger reaction power. It can be attributed to that the as prepared GOQDs with high oxygen content have a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups. In the reduction process of N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT, the N and S atoms can replace the oxygen-containing functional groups on the edge and defect sites of GOQDs to provide active sites for ORR and OER [61,62]. This also provides an idea to increase current density, which is promising to optimize the overall performance of the materials. LSV curves were performed with different annealing-time composites. The composite with GOQDs exhibits a significantly improved electrocatalytic performance compared to the others (Fig. S16). Our experiments prove that thermal treatment increases the graphitic N in the materials and weakens the influence of graphene surface functional groups on the conductivity. However, too long time thermal treatment decreases nitrogen and sulfur contents and reduces the electrocatalytic performance [49,50]. The individual element, N or S-doping slightly distorts the graphene surface and changes the lattice and local charge distribution of graphene. However, using a suitable sulfur source (benzyl disulfide), the doping process is optimized to produce a unique bonding structure (-SO2-) in the material, which promotes the surface adsorption of O2, finally reducing O2 directly to OH− via a 4e- transfer process [53]. Therefore, the preparation of high-performance N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT not only controls the content of graphitized N but also ensures a reasonable level of S-doping.

Therefore, under the best treatment conditions, in order to comprehensively evaluate this metal-free catalyst, we continued to explore what role the addition of nitrogen and sulfur played, respectively. It can be seen in Fig. 4a that the initial potential of N-GOQD-RGO/CNT (~0.90 V) is lower than that of S-GOQD-RGO/CNT (~0.94 V), but N-GOQD-RGO/CNT has a significantly increased limiting current density. We speculate that the introduction of nitrogen heteroatoms can better activate GOQDs to increase the limit of current density. Fig. 4b shows the Tafel plots of all materials and Pt/C. Tafel slope of N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT is 37.93 mV dec−1, far lower than that of Pt/C (64.62 mV dec−1), mainly because GOQDs provide more charge transfer pathways on the graphene/CNT surface, oxygen can be rapidly adsorbed on the surface of composite materials, and the reaction occurs directly through the 4e- pathway. Interestingly, we observed that the Tafel slope of N-GOQD-RGO/CNT is lower than that of S-GOQD-RGO/CNT. These results indicate that nitrogen and sulfur could promote the ORR performance of the catalyst with different degrees, which further proves that nitrogen and sulfur need to cooperate to maximize the effect of exciting GOQDs. Fig. 4c shows half-wave potentials of as-prepared materials and Pt/C. N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT has the highest half-wave potential as ~0.84 V. In summary, N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT has an onset potential and a low Tafel slope, which allows it to retain the highest current density while retaining a positive half-wave potential. LSV curves of N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT at different speeds (400, 625, 900, 1225, and 1600 rpm) were showed in Fig. 4d. It can be seen that N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT exhibits the largest ORR activity and positive onset potential of ~ 0.96 V (close to 1.01 V of Pt/C), the most positive half-wave potential of ~ 0.84 V, and the maximum limiting current density ~ 5.88 mA cm−2. It can also be observed that the small initial fluctuation of the ORR at low rotational speeds is attributed to the lack of oxygen supply. However, as the amount of oxygen increases, it gradually decreases, approaching a stable 4e- process. Limiting current density (JL) is derived from the LSV curves according to Fig. 4d, the relationship between rotational speeds (ω) and K-L relation is obtained using Koutecky – Levich (K-L) equation, and the number of electron transfer (n) is calculated from the slope of the fitted curve in the graph. In Fig. 4e, all K-L curves at potentials of 0.3, 0.4, 0.5, and 0.6 V exhibit a nearly parallel linear relationship, implying the first-order reaction kinetics of the ORR. It is found that N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT has an n value of ~ 4.0, close to Pt/C under the same conditions. It indicates a four-electron oxygen reduction process occurred in N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT composite. In order to investigate effects of GOQDs and heat treatment process on electrocatalytic performances. In addition, Fig. S17 and Fig. S18 show the LSV curves of related materials at different speeds and the corresponding K-L relationship [55,56]. To investigate the charge carrier conductivity of N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT and Pt/C, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were performed. As is shown in the Nyquist plot (Fig. 4f), a very small semicircle at high frequency region represents the charge transfer process at the interface of catalyst and electrolyte. The straight line in the low-frequency region related to ion diffusion resistance. Both the two samples show small semicircle, indicating the high electrocatalytic activity during ORR.

LSV curves of OER conducted in 0.1 M KOH solution are shown in Fig. 5 a and Fig. S19. The OER potential can be judged by EJ = 10. The onset potential of N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT is 1.46 V with a minimum overpotential of 390 mV, much lower than those of Pt/C (500 mV) and IrO2 (430 mV) [51,52]. We performed continuous cycling to investigate the potential stability of N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT. The potential of N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT only decays 10 mV after 5,000 cycles (Fig. S20). Correspondingly, the value of E1/2 for N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT calculated by LSV curves only reduces 10 mV after 5,000 cycles (Fig. 5b). Moreover, the stability of N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT was evaluated by a chronoamperometry, and the ORR stability test was performed at a constant voltage of −0.3 V. The tests above demonstrate outstanding stability of N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT. The catalytic stability of N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT is much higher than that of Pt/C (Fig. 5c). The initial current density retention rate after 18,000 s is ~90%, while that of Pt/C is only 63%. As for the Hg/HgO reference electrode, the initial current density retention rate is ~90% for N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT after 20,000 s (Fig. S21), which is similar to that of Ag/AgCl electrode. For methanol tolerance tests (Fig. 5d), 3 M methanol is rapidly injected into the solution after 300 s, the current of Pt/C instantaneously changes from the cathode ORR current to reverse anode current, implying the occurrence of oxidation reaction of catalytic agent with methanol. However, only small fluctuations are shown for N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT, that is, it has a more robust methanol tolerance than Pt/C. Fig. 5e shows the bifunctional combination of N,S-RGO, N,S-RGO/CNT, N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT and Pt/C (ORR OER) and RuO2 (OER), where bifunctional performance is evaluated with ΔE = EJ=10-EJ=3. Among them, N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT has the lowest ΔE value of 0.78 V, which is better than that of Pt/C (~0.90 V) and even exceeds many materials doped with metals or precious metals (Table S6).

In order to further evaluate the dual performance for ORR and OER, N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT and commercially available Pt/C and RuO2 (mass ratio 1: 1) were used as an air cathode electrocatalyst for zinc-air battery, respectively (Fig. 6 a) [57]. 6 M KOH and 0.2 M Zn(AC)2 were used as the electrolyte. For practical applications, two batteries were series-connected to power a blue light-emitting diode (Fig. S22). The air battery based on N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT can generate a stable open-circuit potential (OCP) of 1.414 V (Fig. 6b), which is close to that based on the Pt/C and Ru/C mixed materials. The discharge polarization curves and power density plots show that the zinc-air battery based on N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT has a higher peak power density (134.3 mW cm−2) than that of Pt/C + Ru/C (100.7 mW cm−2) (Fig. 6c). As shown in Fig. 6d, the zinc-air battery based on N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT has a lower discharge–charge overpotential and exhibits excellent charge-discharge capabilities. Therefore, N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT based zinc-air batteries demonstrate better practical application potential than Pt/C+Ru/C.

In summary, GOQDs were prepared by a one-step cutting method and guided to the 3D framework by rich defect structures on graphene and CNTs. We successfully assembled graphene, GOQDs and CNTs together. The addition of CNTs promotes the optimization of the overall structure of the composite material, prevents the stacking of graphene sheets, and exposes more active sites, while the addition of GOQDs forms a uniform “protrusion” on surfaces of the 3D material and consequently provides more specific reaction areas and promotes charge transport on the material surface. And then, N,S co-doped material was synthesized, where doping level was controlled through a one-step hydrothermal and heat treatment process, leading to the enhancement of synergistic performance of N,S co-doping. Thus, the N,S-GOQD-RGO/CNT composite shows superior half wave potential of 0.84 V, improved current density of 5.88 mA cm−2, lower potential difference of 0.78 V between EORR1/2 and EOER10, and long-term stability. This work will provide new insights for designing electrocatalysts.
